And the answer is that is not a an obligation of that is one of the things that the prophet sallall.aii wasallam would sometimes do and sometimes not do. was set up. Sometimes he would wash his hands first, other times he would not. And the obligations of are the steps mentioned in the sixth verse of the fifth chapter of the Quran in which Allah subhanahu wa ta'ala says These are the four steps and really five because they are mentioned in order. These are the five obligations of Udu and succession that they be done that these all be done together. This is understood from the seak of the verse. This is understood from the way that the verse is structured. So all of them are found there. Washing the hands before beginning woo is something that the prophet would do sall.ai Wasam because of the general reason that whenever he would make wudoo, it's usually because he had relieved himself, right? He would relieve himself and then he would make wudoo and he would always make wudoo right after relieving himself. Rarely would he not make wudoo. And so usually when he is finished relieving himself he would begin to make his wudoo and he would start with what? Washing his hands. The same thing goes when a person wakes up from sleep. It is an obligation that the first thing a person does when he wakes up from sleep is to wash his hands or to wash our hands. The prophet sallall.aii wasallam says He says because while you were asleep, you don't know where your hands have been. You don't know where your hands have been. So the sunnah is to always wash your hands and sense the prophet sallall.aii wasallam would usually be making wudoo after relieving himself or after waking up. He would wash his hands before he would start with the wudoo. But the first obligation of wubul is washing the face. And we said part of the face is what? The mouth and the nose. How would he do this? He would take the water in his right hand, cup it this. He would take them together, half into his mouth and half into his nose. And then he would blow it out with his left hand. And al meaning he would move the water around in his mouth and then spit it out. He would do this three times or two times or at least one time. All are correct. The obligatory is once. The sunnah is two or three times. But the bare minimum one time. And what about what we see people do sometimes? You know, what people do is often times what gets worked into other people's other people's actions. And it's it's a it's a double-edged sword, right? What is something that if I want you to buy a product, one of the things really one of the six universal things that if I show you, you'll be more likely to buy my product. Well, I show you other people doing it. If I show you other people buying my product, it shortcircuits something. It's a shortcut to decision making that human beings have. Why? Because we assume being that human beings are intelligent creatures that they are not going to do something without thinking it through, without having evidence. But what we fail to realize is that me seeing someone do something and doing it just because he did it and assuming the burden of proof was on him, he could have been doing exactly what I'm doing and that is copying the other guy, the next guy, assuming that the next guy had the evidence, right? But that doesn't occur to us. So human beings naturally when they see people want something or do something immediately it starts becoming interesting. All right. summary: This lecture focuses on the proper method of performing wudoo (ablution) according to Islamic teachings, specifically addressing common misconceptions and practices that deviate from the Sunnah of the Prophet Muhammad. The speaker explains the five obligatory steps of wudoo as mentioned in the Quran (Chapter 5, Verse 6), emphasizing that washing the face is the first obligation. He clarifies that washing hands before beginning wudoo is recommended but not obligatory, and discusses the correct method of washing the mouth and nose together in one action rather than separately. The speaker also addresses the cultural phenomenon of how people adopt practices they see others doing without verifying their Islamic validity, warning against this approach in matters of religious worship.
suggested_title: The Correct Method of Wudoo According to the Sunnah: Clarifying Common Misconceptions
